name: c4-modeling
description: C4 architectural modeling for documenting software architecture. Use when creating architecture diagrams, planning new systems, communicating with stakeholders, or conducting architecture reviews.
capabilities: Generate C4 Context diagrams showing system boundaries and external actors
Create C4 Container diagrams with technology stack specifications
Build C4 Component diagrams detailing internal module structures
Provide level-appropriate guidance to prevent abstraction mixing
Reference comprehensive Mermaid syntax for all C4 diagram types
author: Consiliency
securitySummary: This skill provides documentation and reference materials for creating C4 architecture diagrams using Mermaid syntax. All 114 static findings are false positives: backticks in Markdown code blocks were incorrectly flagged as shell commands, and the text C4 was misidentified as cryptographic references. The skill contains no executable code, network operations, or filesystem access.
valueStatement: Software architecture diagrams are often inconsistent and hard to maintain across teams. This skill provides structured guidance for creating clear, hierarchical architecture diagrams using the C4 model methodology with Mermaid syntax.
userTitle: Create C4 Architecture Diagrams with Mermaid
limitations: Does not generate diagrams automatically, provides syntax and guidance only
Requires manual creation of diagrams using Mermaid-compatible tools
Limited to C4 methodology, not suitable for other architecture frameworks
Does not validate diagram correctness or architectural decisions

useCases.title: New System Architecture Planning
useCases.description: Design and document architecture for a new microservices platform starting with context diagrams and progressively zooming into container and component levels.

useCases.title: Legacy System Documentation
useCases.description: Create comprehensive architecture documentation for existing systems to improve team understanding and onboarding.

useCases.title: Stakeholder Communication
useCases.description: Generate appropriate-level diagrams for different audiences, from executive context diagrams to detailed component views for developers.

promptTemplates.title: Create Context Diagram
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a C4 Context diagram for an e-commerce platform showing customers, admins, and external payment and shipping systems
promptTemplates.scenario: Starting architecture documentation for a new project

promptTemplates.title: Generate Container Diagram
promptTemplates.prompt: Generate a C4 Container diagram for the API server showing web app, mobile app, database, cache, and message queue with technology choices
promptTemplates.scenario: Documenting the technical architecture for developers

promptTemplates.title: Build Component Diagram
promptTemplates.prompt: Build a C4 Component diagram for the order processing service showing repositories, business logic, and external integrations
promptTemplates.scenario: Detailing internal structure of a critical service

promptTemplates.title: Review Diagram Levels
promptTemplates.prompt: Review my C4 diagram to verify I am not mixing abstraction levels and that all elements have appropriate descriptions and technology specifications

bestPractices: Always start at Level 1 Context diagram before creating detailed Container or Component diagrams to establish the big picture
Never mix abstraction levels in a single diagram such as showing both containers and components together
Include technology choices and clear descriptions for all elements at Level 2 and above to provide useful documentation
antiPatterns: Creating a Container diagram without first having a Context diagram that shows where the system fits in its environment
Showing implementation details like specific classes or functions in Context or Container level diagrams
Creating Code level diagrams for simple CRUD operations or frequently changing code that will quickly become outdated

faq.question: What is the C4 model?
faq.answer: The C4 model is a hierarchical approach to software architecture diagrams with four levels: Context shows the system in its environment, Container shows major components, Component shows internal structure, and Code shows implementation details.

faq.question: When should I use each C4 level?
faq.answer: Use Context for all stakeholders to show the big picture, Container for technical teams to show architecture, Component for developers working on specific parts, and Code sparingly for complex areas requiring detailed documentation.

faq.question: Can I mix different levels in one diagram?
faq.answer: No, mixing abstraction levels creates confusion. Each diagram should focus on one level. Create separate diagrams for different zoom levels and link them together.

faq.question: What tools can render Mermaid C4 diagrams?
faq.answer: Mermaid diagrams can be rendered in GitHub, GitLab, Visual Studio Code with extensions, Notion, Confluence, and many documentation tools that support Mermaid syntax.

faq.question: How many elements should be in a C4 diagram?
faq.answer: Context diagrams should have 5 to 10 elements, Container diagrams 10 to 15, and Component diagrams 10 to 20. If you exceed these counts, consider splitting into multiple diagrams.

faq.question: Do I need to create all four levels?
faq.answer: No, most systems only need Context and Container levels. Use Component for complex areas and Code level very sparingly for critical logic that requires detailed documentation.
